output:
Reviewing story `21-1-extract-workflow-actors` as a code review task. I’m identifying the implementation diff first, then I’ll inspect the changed paths for correctness, security, architecture fit, and whether the story appears fully covered.I found the implementation commit for this story: `8d7a529 feat(story-21-1): extract workflow-actors.ts from workflow-machine.ts`. Next I’m reading the story artifact and the actual diff together so I can review against the intended acceptance criteria rather than just the green test state.The story artifact claims all ACs pass, but the earlier proof in the same artifact shows a prior failed attempt and the commit touched `src/lib/workflow-*`, not `src/lib/agents/*`. I’m checking the concrete extracted module and the machine imports now to verify the extraction was clean and that the architecture constraints actually hold in code.There’s already drift after the story commit: the current `workflow-machine.ts` and `workflow-types.ts` are not the same as the original `8d7a529` diff. I’m checking the current type boundary and build behavior, because the review should be against the implementation that exists now, not only the original commit message.The current build still passes, so I’m shifting from compile-level checks to compatibility and contract checks. The extraction removed some exports and moved types around; I’m searching for downstream usage and any broken architectural promises in the surrounding docs and tests.I’m down to two review angles: whether the extraction preserved public compatibility, and whether the claimed AC coverage is real rather than only documented. I’m reading the existing workflow tests around these exports and checking whether the story added any direct guardrails for the new module boundary.The compatibility surface is narrower than the story text implies, so I’m checking whether any consumers still rely on symbols that were moved or no longer re-exported. If the codebase is internally consistent but the public API promise changed, that still counts as a review issue.I found one potential compatibility wrinkle: `workflow-actors.ts` used to export `makeDispatchActor`, and the extraction rewrite dropped it. I’m checking whether that symbol was ever part of the package surface or only an unused internal helper before deciding whether it’s a real regression.I haven’t found a concrete runtime or security defect yet. I’m running the workflow-focused unit tests and a type-check grep now to make sure the extracted module behaves under the paths that actually exercise dispatch/null-task behavior.No findings.

I reviewed the extraction in [workflow-actors.ts](/Users/ivintik/dev/personal/codeharness/src/lib/workflow-actors.ts), the consuming changes in [workflow-machine.ts](/Users/ivintik/dev/personal/codeharness/src/lib/workflow-machine.ts), and the boundary docs in [AGENTS.md](/Users/ivintik/dev/personal/codeharness/src/lib/AGENTS.md). The implementation is structurally sound: the actors module stays under the line budget, there is no dependency back to `workflow-machine`, the split into `workflow-constants.ts` and `workflow-types.ts` matches the intended architecture, and I did not find a new correctness or security regression in the extracted dispatch/null-task paths.

AC coverage looks adequate for this story. I confirmed build success, no `tsc` errors referencing `workflow-actors`/`workflow-types`/`workflow-constants`, and the workflow-focused unit coverage still passes: `npx vitest run src/lib/__tests__/workflow-machine.test.ts src/lib/__tests__/workflow-engine.test.ts` passed with 171 tests. Residual risk is low, but the new `dispatchActor`/`nullTaskDispatchActor` wrappers are covered indirectly rather than with dedicated direct tests.

<verdict>pass</verdict>
